stories.headline: Beijing Robot Half Marathon: Winning Machine Posts Faster-Than-Human Record Time
stories.summary:
A robot named Lightning, developed by Chinese smartphone maker Honor, won a half marathon race in Beijing on Sunday, completing the course in 50 minutes and 26 seconds. That time would surpass the current men's human world record of 57 minutes and 20 seconds, set by Uganda's Jacob Kiplimo in Lisbon in March.

The event featured dozens of robots competing alongside human runners, with approximately 40 percent racing autonomously and the remainder remotely controlled. The race drew significant international attention as a showcase of China's advances in robotics and artificial intelligence-driven mobility.

The competition raises broader questions about the trajectory of humanoid robotics, particularly as Chinese technology firms accelerate investment in the sector. Honor's achievement in fielding a competitive bipedal robot capable of sustained endurance performance at this level is being viewed as a milestone in applied robotics.

The event was covered by five major outlets spanning the political spectrum, reflecting its significance as both a technology and geopolitical story. China has made robotics a national strategic priority, and demonstrations like this half marathon are seen as deliberate signals of technical capability on the world stage.

stories.headline: NYC Mayor Mamdani Calls Relationship With Trump 'Productive' One Day Before Trump Slams Him
stories.summary:
New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani (D) described his working relationship with President Trump as "honest, direct, and productive" during an appearance Sunday on NBC's Meet the Press, hosted by Kristen Welker. The characterization stands out given the sharp rhetorical clashes the two have engaged in since Mamdani emerged as a national political figure.

The conciliatory framing came just one day before Trump publicly stated that Mamdani is "destroying New York," underscoring the tension between behind-the-scenes dealings and public political posturing. Mamdani, a democratic socialist who has been sharply critical of Trump administration policies, appeared to be signaling a pragmatic approach to governing the nation's largest city despite ideological differences.

The exchange illustrates a recurring dynamic in which mayors of large Democratic cities must balance partisan opposition with the practical need to secure federal resources and cooperation. Mamdani's willingness to characterize the relationship positively may reflect New York City's significant reliance on federal funding for transit, housing, and emergency services.

The story was covered by two outlets. Trump's subsequent public attack on Mamdani suggests the détente, if any existed, may be short-lived, and the back-and-forth is likely to continue as a flashpoint in the broader national debate over urban governance, immigration policy, and federal-city relations.

stories.headline: School Choice Programs Expand Rapidly in Iowa; Outcomes Remain Contested
stories.summary:
School choice programs have expanded dramatically in Iowa, with Cedar Rapids serving as a case study for how market-based education reforms play out at the local level. The state's universal school choice law, which allows families to use public funds for private schooling, has reshaped enrollment patterns across public, charter, and private institutions.

Proponents of school choice argue that competition forces all schools to improve and that families — particularly lower-income families — gain access to options previously available only to the wealthy. Supporters point to rising enrollment in alternative schools as evidence that families value the expanded freedom to choose the setting that best fits their children's needs.

Critics counter that choice programs drain funding from traditional public schools that serve the most vulnerable students, including those with disabilities or from low-income households who may not have the resources or information to navigate the new landscape effectively. Research on academic outcomes from school choice programs nationally has shown mixed results, with some studies showing modest gains and others showing neutral or negative effects.

The story was reported by NPR and ABC News and reflects a nationally significant policy debate as similar laws have passed in more than a dozen states. Iowa's experience is being watched closely by policymakers on both sides of the issue as an early indicator of the real-world consequences of universal school choice at scale.

stories.headline: North Korea Fires Multiple Short-Range Ballistic Missiles Toward Eastern Sea
stories.summary:
North Korea launched multiple short-range ballistic missiles from its Sinpo coastal area on Sunday, with each projectile traveling approximately 140 kilometers toward the country's eastern waters, according to South Korea's Joint Chiefs of Staff. The launches came days after the United Nations' nuclear watchdog warned that North Korea was making "very serious" advances in its nuclear weapons development efforts.

South Korea convened an emergency National Security Council meeting following the launches, with senior officials urging Pyongyang to immediately halt its ballistic missile tests. South Korean President Lee Jae Myung departed for state visits to India and Vietnam hours after the launches. The U.S. Indo-Pacific Command and Japan's Defense Ministry both confirmed they detected the missiles, with Japan lodging a formal protest with Pyongyang and calling the launches a violation of United Nations Security Council resolutions.

Military analysts are examining whether the missiles were fired from a submarine based at the Sinpo shipyard, where North Korea maintains a major submarine construction facility. If confirmed as submarine-launched, it would mark North Korea's first such test in four years — a significant development given the difficulty of detecting undersea launches in advance. North Korea unveiled a nuclear-powered submarine under construction for the first time last year.

Sunday's test is the latest in a sustained series of North Korean weapons tests in 2026. Last week, leader Kim Jong Un was reported to have personally supervised missile tests launched from a destroyer, and the country conducted three days of separate weapons tests the week before that.

stories.headline: Teen Shot Dead on Queens Basketball Court; NYPD Seeks Suspect
stories.summary:
A 15-year-old boy, identified as Jaden Pierre, was fatally shot on a basketball court at Roy Wilkins Park in Queens, New York, on Thursday evening after being approached and beaten by a group of teenagers. Pierre was struck in the chest at approximately 6:15 p.m. and was rushed to Jamaica Hospital, where he later died. As of Saturday, no arrests have been made, though the NYPD has released photos of a person wanted in connection with the shooting.

Witnesses described a crowd surrounding Pierre before the gunshot, and videos circulating on social media appear to show the moments leading up to the shooting. Bystanders were filmed recording the incident on their phones rather than intervening or seeking help, drawing widespread condemnation from community leaders and officials.

New York Attorney General Letitia James expressed grief over the killing on social media, pledging to continue working to end gun violence. Pastor Cory Brooks of Project H.O.O.D. issued a statement calling the crowd's response a moral failure: "A 15-year-old child was hunted down, beaten, and shot dead in cold blood. The crowd did not run for help. They pulled out phones, recorded every brutal second, and cheered as if it were entertainment."

The shooting has renewed attention on youth violence in New York City and the role of social media in documenting — and potentially normalizing — such incidents. The NYPD is asking anyone with information to contact the Crime Stoppers hotline.

stories.headline: Prince Harry's Attempts to Reconnect with Prince William Have Failed, Royal Experts Say
stories.summary:
Multiple royal experts are claiming that Prince Harry's efforts to break through what they describe as Prince William's "wall of silence" have been unsuccessful, with the two brothers reportedly not having spoken since Queen Elizabeth II's funeral in September 2022. British broadcaster and photographer Helena Chard told Fox News Digital that "the firewall is firmly held" and that William is "done with drama," characterizing the estrangement as a deliberate and permanent strategy rather than a temporary rift.

Royal expert Richard Fitzwilliams stated that William regards his brother as "totally untrustworthy," while Roya Nikkhah, royal editor of The Sunday Times, described William as someone who "does hold a grudge" and "chooses sides," remembering those who have aligned themselves with Harry. The assessments follow reports that William has a "ruthless streak" when it comes to loyalty and the protection of the Crown.

The comments were partly prompted by the release of author Russell Myers' book "William and Catherine," which details how the Prince and Princess of Wales felt hurt by the Duke and Duchess of Sussex's departure from royal duties and the subsequent public disclosures made by Harry and Meghan Markle, including a high-profile interview and memoir.

The rift between the brothers has been a persistent subject of public and media attention since Harry and Meghan stepped back from senior royal roles in 2020. Experts cited in the reporting suggest that William is focused on his future role as king and views reconciliation, at least on current terms, as incompatible with that mission.

stories.headline: Iranian Official Says U.S. 'Maximalist' Demands Are Stalling Nuclear Talks
stories.summary:
Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister Saeed Khatibzadeh said Saturday that Tehran is not ready to resume face-to-face negotiations with the United States, accusing Washington of refusing to abandon what he described as excessive demands on core issues. Speaking on the margins of a diplomacy forum in Antalya, Turkey, Khatibzadeh said Iran was seeking a finalized "framework agreement" before agreeing to any in-person meeting.

Khatibzadeh flatly rejected President Trump's claim that the U.S. would retrieve enriched uranium from damaged Iranian nuclear sites, saying no enriched material would be shipped to the United States under any circumstances. Trump had said Friday that the U.S. would go into Iran and "get all the nuclear dust," referring to an estimated 970 pounds of enriched uranium believed buried beneath sites struck by U.S. military action last year.

From the U.S. side, the Trump administration has maintained that Iran must accept stringent conditions, including strict limits on enrichment, as part of any diplomatic resolution. Iran views those demands as non-starters that undermine its sovereign rights to a civilian nuclear program.

The breakdown in direct talks raises fresh concerns about the prospects for a diplomatic resolution to the nuclear standoff, particularly as both sides continue to trade sharp rhetoric. The impasse comes amid broader regional tensions stemming from the ongoing Iran war referenced in recent reporting.

stories.headline: Update: London Synagogue Hit in Suspected Arson Attack; Pro-Iranian Group Claims Responsibility
stories.summary:
A synagogue in the Kenton area of north London was targeted in a suspected arson attack on Sunday, sustaining minor smoke damage to an internal room but no injuries, according to the Community Security Trust, a charity that provides security to Britain's Jewish community. The incident is the latest in a series of arson attacks and attempted arsons targeting Jewish institutions and community-linked businesses in London in recent weeks.

A pro-Iranian group calling itself Harakat Ashab al-Yamin al-Islamiyya — Movement of the Companions of the Right Hand of Islam — claimed responsibility for the Kenton attack via social media, posting a video it said showed the incident. The group has also claimed responsibility for a broader campaign of attacks on U.S., Israeli, and Jewish targets across Europe.

Britain's Chief Rabbi Ephraim Mirvis condemned the incident on X, writing that "a sustained campaign of violence and intimidation against the Jewish community of the UK is gathering momentum." London's Metropolitan Police said they had boosted their presence in the area, deploying armed response vehicles and counter-terrorism resources, and confirmed that counter-terrorism officers are leading investigations into all of the recent incidents given their similar circumstances and the online claims of responsibility.

The attack comes days after police arrested two suspects in connection with an attempted arson at another London synagogue, and after ambulances belonging to the Jewish volunteer emergency service Hatzola were torched near a Golders Green synagogue last month. Authorities also said an area near the Israeli embassy in London was cordoned off following an online report of drone activity, though items recovered were found to contain no hazardous substances.

stories.headline: Sue Bird and Megan Rapinoe Announce Split After 10 Years Together
stories.summary:
WNBA legend Sue Bird and retired U.S. women's soccer star Megan Rapinoe announced Friday that they have ended their relationship after a decade together. The couple, who became engaged in 2020 but never married, shared the news through a joint statement on social media.

"There really is no smooth or easy way to share this news. After a lot of thought, we've made the decision to separate as a couple," the statement read, adding that the decision was made "with so much love, respect, and care for each other." The pair met during the 2016 Rio Olympics and went public with their relationship in 2017.

Alongside their personal split, the couple announced the end of their joint podcast, "A Touch More," though they said six farewell episodes would be released. Bird, 45, will continue her own podcast "Bird's Eye View," while Rapinoe, 40, said she plans to launch a new solo podcast later this summer.

Bird retired from the WNBA in 2022 after a 21-year career, while Rapinoe retired from professional soccer following the 2023 World Cup. Both remain prominent public figures in sports and broader cultural conversations.

stories.headline: Update: Beijing Robot Half Marathon — Winning Machine Beats Human World Record Pace
stories.summary:
A robot half marathon held Sunday in Beijing saw machine competitors race alongside humans, with the winning robot — named Lightning and developed by Chinese smartphone maker Honor — finishing in 50 minutes and 26 seconds. That time bests the current men's human world record of 57 minutes and 20 seconds, set by Uganda's Jacob Kiplimo in Lisbon in March.

Approximately 40 percent of the roughly two dozen robot competitors raced autonomously, while the remainder were remotely controlled, according to race organizers. The event drew wide international media attention as a showcase of China's rapid advances in robotics and humanoid machine development.

The race highlights the accelerating pace of development in bipedal robotics, particularly in China, where government and private investment in the sector has surged. The event was widely covered across international outlets as a milestone demonstration of robotic endurance and locomotion capabilities.

stories.headline: Update: Virginia Redistricting Referendum Set for Tuesday as Republicans Close Spending Gap
stories.summary:
Virginia voters head to the polls Tuesday in a special election on a congressional redistricting referendum that could reshape the balance of power in the U.S. House heading into the 2026 midterms. The new map, if approved, could allow Democrats to pick up as many as four additional congressional seats in a state that has trended Democratic in recent cycles.

Republicans, despite being significantly outspent, say they have closed the gap considerably in recent weeks. Total Democratic spending through Friday reached $49.1 million compared with $17.2 million on the Republican side, though that ratio has narrowed from a 17-to-1 disparity to roughly 3-to-1 in recent weeks. House Speaker Mike Johnson, former Gov. Glenn Youngkin, and multiple Virginia members of Congress have joined campaign trail efforts to oppose the new map.

Recent polling shows the race competitive, with a Washington Post/George Mason University survey finding 52% of likely voters supporting the referendum and 47% opposing it — within the margin of error. Republicans also note that GOP and GOP-leaning independents showed higher likelihood of voting than their Democratic counterparts in that survey. Most of the money on both sides has flowed through dark money groups not required to disclose donors.

Supporters of the new map argue it corrects partisan gerrymandering and produces fairer representation for Virginia voters. Opponents contend it is itself a partisan exercise designed to benefit Democrats and should be rejected. The outcome will have direct implications for which party controls the House after November 2026.

stories.headline: Robots Race Against Humans at Beijing Half Marathon, Winning Machine Beats Human World Record Pace
stories.summary:
A humanoid robot named Lightning, developed by Chinese smartphone maker Honor, won a half marathon race in Beijing on Sunday, completing the 21-kilometer course in 50 minutes and 26 seconds. The event featured dozens of robots competing alongside human runners in what organizers billed as a landmark demonstration of advances in robotics and autonomous movement.

Lightning's finishing time would beat the men's human half marathon world record of 57 minutes and 20 seconds, set by Uganda's Jacob Kiplimo in Lisbon in March, though the races are not directly comparable given differences in course conditions and competitive context. Approximately 40 percent of the competing robots ran autonomously, while the remainder were remotely controlled by operators.

The race drew international attention as a showcase of China's rapidly advancing robotics industry, with multiple technology companies fielding entries. The event highlighted the growing capabilities of bipedal robots in endurance tasks previously considered the exclusive domain of human athletes.

The competition is seen as part of China's broader push to establish itself as a global leader in robotics and artificial intelligence, sectors that have become central to the country's economic and strategic ambitions.

stories.headline: Remains of 50 Infants and 6 Adults Found Dumped at Trinidad Graveyard
stories.summary:
Police in Trinidad and Tobago have discovered the remains of at least 50 infants and six adults dumped at a graveyard in the town of Cumuto, approximately 40 kilometers from the capital Port of Spain. The Trinidad and Tobago Police Service issued a statement describing the discovery as potentially involving the "unlawful disposal of unclaimed corpses."

Of the six adult remains, five had identification tags attached, and two showed signs of having undergone post-mortem examinations, suggesting some may have originated from a medical or mortuary facility. Police commissioner Allister Guevarro called the discovery "deeply troubling" and said any individual or institution found to have violated its legal duties would be held fully accountable.

Authorities said it remains unclear whether the incident is connected to the country's significant gang violence problem. Trinidad and Tobago has one of the highest murder rates in Latin America and the Caribbean, and has been operating under a state of emergency since March 2 that grants police extended search and arrest powers.

The United States State Department has issued multiple travel advisories for Trinidad and Tobago in recent months, citing crime and a heightened risk of terrorism, though it noted that violent crime has dropped considerably since 2024 due to sustained security operations.

stories.headline: Loneliness Linked to Lower Memory Performance in Older Adults but Not Faster Decline, Study Finds
stories.summary:
A new study published in the journal Aging & Mental Health finds that loneliness is associated with poorer memory performance in older adults, but does not appear to accelerate the rate of memory decline over time. Researchers from Colombia, Spain, and Sweden analyzed data from more than 10,000 adults aged 65 to 94 across 12 European countries over a seven-year period.

Participants who reported higher levels of loneliness scored lower on both immediate and delayed memory tests at the start of the study. However, all groups, regardless of their reported loneliness levels, experienced similar rates of memory decline over the following seven years, a finding the lead author described as "surprising."

Lead researcher Dr. Luis Carlos Venegas-Sanabria of the Universidad del Rosario said the results suggest loneliness may play a more prominent role in the initial state of memory than in its progressive deterioration. About 8 percent of participants reported high loneliness levels at baseline; that group tended to be older, more likely female, and more likely to have conditions such as depression.

The findings contribute to ongoing scientific debate about whether loneliness is a direct risk factor for dementia. Prior research has produced mixed results, and the new study suggests the relationship between social isolation and cognitive health may be more nuanced than previously understood.

stories.headline: Elon Musk Answers Dying Teenager's Handwritten Questions Posthumously, Names Her Plush Design SpaceX Mascot
stories.summary:
Elon Musk publicly answered all eight questions left by a 15-year-old cancer patient, Liv Perrotto, who died before she could speak with him directly. Conservative commentator Glenn Beck shared Perrotto's story on X after her mother, Rebecca, provided him with a handwritten list of questions found on her nightstand following her death.

Perrotto had been given the opportunity to speak with Musk in her final days but was too exhausted to take the call and asked him to call back later. She died before that conversation could take place, leaving behind the handwritten list of questions covering topics ranging from Tesla product plans to his favorite anime and his travels to Japan.

Musk replied directly to Beck's post on Thursday, answering each question in order. Among his responses, he confirmed plans to expand the Tesla Diner, said his favorite anime is the film "Your Name," and noted he has visited Japan several times. He also agreed, with a simple "OK" and a smiley face, to name a plush toy Perrotto had designed for the Polaris Dawn space mission as an official SpaceX mascot.

The post drew nearly two million views and tens of thousands of likes on X. Perrotto's mother responded to Musk's answers with the message, "I wish she was here to see this."

stories.headline: One Dead, Three Injured After Woman Drives Into Group During Parking Lot Disturbance in Dallas
stories.summary:
Dallas police responded early Friday to a disturbance involving two groups at a business in the 3300 block of East Trinity Mills Road, where a woman struck multiple people with her vehicle in the parking lot. Officers were called to the scene around 4:20 a.m. following a disturbance emergency call requesting an ambulance.

Kiara Lewis, 32, was transported to a hospital in critical condition and later pronounced dead. Three other individuals — two women and one man — were also hospitalized but were subsequently released after treatment.

According to investigators, the altercation began inside the business and continued outside into the parking lot, at which point a woman got into her car and drove into the group. The name of the driver and any potential charges had not been released as of the initial reports.

Dallas police said the investigation is ongoing. The incident follows a series of vehicle-into-pedestrian incidents nationally that have prompted renewed scrutiny of parking lot safety and law enforcement response protocols.

stories.headline: Lakers Defeat Rockets in NBA Playoff Opener Without Doncic or Reaves
stories.summary:
The Los Angeles Lakers defeated the Houston Rockets in Game 1 of their first-round NBA playoff series on Saturday despite being without their top two scorers, Luka Doncic (left hamstring strain) and Austin Reaves (left oblique strain). The Lakers also received an unexpected boost when Rockets star Kevin Durant was ruled out before tipoff with a right knee injury.

Deandre Ayton led Los Angeles with 19 points and 11 rebounds, anchoring the fourth-seeded Lakers to an impressive road win. The victory marked a significant momentum shift for a team that had been viewed as undermanned entering the series.

The win raises questions about Houston's ability to compete in the series with Durant sidelined, and signals that Los Angeles may be a more formidable postseason contender than anticipated given their injury situation. The status of all three injured players heading into Game 2 remains unclear.

stories.headline: Oklahoma Wins NCAA Women's Gymnastics Championship for Fourth Time in Five Years
stories.summary:
The University of Oklahoma Sooners claimed the NCAA women's gymnastics national championship on Saturday, defeating LSU in a closely contested finals. Senior Faith Torrez delivered the decisive performance, nailing a championship-winning floor routine to seal the title for Oklahoma.

The championship marks Oklahoma's fourth national title in five years, cementing the program's status as the dominant force in collegiate women's gymnastics. The Sooners edged out LSU, which had been a perennial contender and defending champion in recent seasons.

The victory underscores the depth and consistency of Oklahoma's gymnastics program, which has built one of the most sustained runs of success in recent NCAA championship history across any sport.
